coredata.dc:title: Electrochemically driven inactivation of harmful cyanobacteria assisted with the visible light photocatalyst of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs

coredata.dc:description: The application of photoelectrocatalysis in the inactivation of algae is attracting growing attention. In this study, CeO2-Fe3O4-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared as particle electrodes to assess their effectiveness in inactivating cyanobacteria within a three-dimensional (3D) electrochemical system under visible light. The results showed that both algal cells and extracellular organic matter (EOM) could be efficiently removed. The incorporation of 10 mg/mL CQDs significantly boosts the efficiency of photoelectrocatalysis under the optimal conditions, where the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs concentration is 0.20 g/L, j = 21 mA/cm2, under a neutral pH. Notably, the CeO₂-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibit favorable electrochemical catalytic performance, outstanding photocurrent responsiveness, and efficient magnetic separation (for recyclability). The coexistence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ species gives rise to the formation of a redox couple (Ce3+/Ce4+) at the interface of CeO₂, thereby demonstrating favorable performance in terms of oxygen release and storage capabilities. The activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and malondialdehyde (MDA) confirm the apoptosis of algal cells. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O₂) are participated in the algae inactivation process, with 1O₂ playing a more prominent role. Our research clarifies the complex mechanisms of algal apoptosis and promotes practical algae inactivation from wastewater.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The application of photoelectrocatalysis in the inactivation of algae is attracting growing attention. In this study, CeO2-Fe3O4-carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were prepared as particle electrodes to assess their effectiveness in inactivating cyanobacteria within a three-dimensional (3D) electrochemical system under visible light. The results showed that both algal cells and extracellular organic matter (EOM) could be efficiently removed. The incorporation of 10 mg/mL CQDs significantly boosts the efficiency of photoelectrocatalysis under the optimal conditions, where the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs concentration is 0.20 g/L, j = 21 mA/cm2, under a neutral pH. Notably, the CeO₂-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibit favorable electrochemical catalytic performance, outstanding photocurrent responsiveness, and efficient magnetic separation (for recyclability). The coexistence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ species gives rise to the formation of a redox couple (Ce3+/Ce4+) at the interface of CeO₂, thereby demonstrating favorable performance in terms of oxygen release and storage capabilities. The activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT), and malondialdehyde (MDA) confirm the apoptosis of algal cells. Hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and singlet oxygen (1O₂) are participated in the algae inactivation process, with 1O₂ playing a more prominent role. Our research clarifies the complex mechanisms of algal apoptosis and promotes practical algae inactivation from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The rampant growth of cyanobacteria blooms in natural water bodies has evolved into a highly pressing concern. A recent research study has revealed that during the period from 2003 to 2020, the spatial coverage of algal blooms in eutrophic waters has expanded by a staggering 13.2%, and their occurrence frequency has skyrocketed by 59.2%. This phenomenon is not confined to a particular region but is a global issue (Vasseghian et al., 2024). As photosynthetic microorganisms, algae thrive by harnessing light energy and nutrients present in water (Sutherland and Burke, 2023). The surge in algal growth has led to a significant disruption of the ecological equilibrium within water bodies, resulting in a decline in water quality. Moreover, it poses risks to both fishery resources and human health (Tian et al., 2021). Microcystis aeruginosa is a quintessential cyanobacterium and prokaryote. It is a widely distributed freshwater alga with remarkable adaptability, especially in eutrophic habitats.Notably, Microcystis aeruginosa is a quintessential cyanobacterium and a prokaryotic organism (Zhang et al., 2023a,b).

Physical strategies for the removal of algae, such as mechanical screening, ultrasonic disruption, and adsorption, have been widely employed. However, their highly labor-intensive characteristics generally limit their overall effectiveness (Greeshma et al., 2022). Chemical methods are also commonly used, which involve the utilization of oxidants, disinfectants, and various chemicals to deactivate algae. Unfortunately, these treatments carry the potential of causing substantial secondary environmental pollution. It is worth noting that coagulation has received a lot of attention as a physicochemical method. Nevertheless, it is vulnerable to environmental factors and is encumbered by the drawback of incurring relatively high costs (Zhang et al., 2023a,b). Biological methods for controlling algae, including the introduction of organisms that suppress or decompose algae, are under investigation as potential alternatives. Although they hold the promise of providing long-term solutions, these approaches necessitate longer treatment durations (Yang et al., 2020).

Electrochemical technology is emerging as a cost-effective and efficient solution for deep water treatment. It outperforms conventional methods by streamlining operations and lowering the costs associated with pollutant removal (Acosta-Santoyo et al., 2024; Jiang et al., 2022). Zhou et al. have illustrated that the Boron-Doped Diamond (BDD) electrode can effectively inactivate cyanobacteria, thereby highlighting the potential of electrochemistry in the realm of algae control (Zhou et al., 2018). Three-dimensional particle electrodes have been proven to enhance the degradation of electrochemical pollutants. Their compact structure, large specific surface area, and improved hydrodynamic characteristics result in elevated reaction rates and enhanced efficiencies (Liu et al., 2023a,b). They also overcome the limitations related to electrode surface area and mitigate electrode surface contamination (H. Li et al., 2021). Notably, Zhao et al. utilized iron-doped biochar as catalytic particle electrodes in combination with the three-dimensional electro-Fenton method to enhance the removal of Ni-EDTA (Zhao et al., 2023). In our prior research, granular activated carbon (GAC) was adopted as a 3D granular electrode in a permonosulfate (PMS)/Cl−-PbO2/Ti system to augment ammonia oxidation and N2 conversion, attaining a conversion efficiency of 71.5% within 90 min (Zhang et al., 2024).

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), nano-sized carbon materials resembling crystal quantum dots in appearance (Zhong et al., 2023), possess distinctive physical and chemical properties. These include facile synthesis, low toxicity, high chemical and thermal stability, as well as remarkable photoelectronic characteristics (Latha et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2023a,b). Such properties have enabled their wide-ranging applications in biomedical and optoelectronic materials (Desmond et al., 2021; Saikia et al., 2023). Ming et al. activated peroxymonosulfate via photocatalysis with functional carbon nitride and carbon quantum dots, effectively degrading bisphenol An under visible light. Incorporating carbon quantum dots into functional polymer carbon nitride can increase its surface area, improve light absorption and charge separation, and enhance photocatalytic activation (Ming et al., 2021).

Cerium dioxide (CeO2) represents a crucial catalyst endowed with high catalytic activity, favorable photoelectric attributes, and a large specific surface area (Rani et al., 2024; Xiao et al., 2024). CeO2 nanorods adorned with small Pt nanoparticles (with a size of 3.46 nm) have been demonstrated to possess excellent electrocatalytic capabilities for the methanol oxidation reaction. The existence of Ce3+ and these nanoparticles fortifies metal interactions and augments the formation of oxygen vacancies on the CeO2 surface (Ravichandran et al., 2024). Interestingly, Li et al. developed a CeO2@CNTs/CdSe composite that enhances adsorption and photocatalysis for water pollutant removal. The results showed that CeO2@CNTs/CdSe composite demonstrated effective removal of Congo red, with 83.58% degradation, and nearly complete Cr(VI) reduction (Li et al., 2023). Furthermore, Fe3O4 features remarkable electrical conductivity, catalytic efficacy, and magnetic characteristics. Its stability and magnetic separability make it suitable for 3D particle electrode construction (Dang et al., 2023). In practical applications, Zhu et al. synthesized a Z-type CuO/Fe3O4 heterojunction graphene oxide and probed its capacity to remove tetracycline under photocatalytic circumstances. The results demonstrated that the decomposition efficiency of tetracycline was approximately 97.3% under the optimal treatment conditions (Zhu et al., 2022). Meanwhile, Masoumi et al. explored the removal of diazinon from aqueous solutions by loading ferromagnetite nanoparticles onto microbial cellulose (MC) as particle electrodes. The nanocomposites functioned as catalysts to activate persulfate, generating sulfate radicals to expedite the removal of diazinon from the solutions (Masoumi et al., 2023).

This study aims to enhance the efficiency of electrochemical cyanobacterial inactivation using three-dimensional CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particle electrodes under visible light. It addresses the issues of low algal inactivation efficiency and high energy use in existing electrochemical methods. The optimal parameters for algal inactivation by the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs three-dimensional electrochemical system will also be determined, and the underlying mechanism of cyanobacterial inactivation will be explored. These results would be contributed to the potential application of electrochemical techniques in the treatment of cyanobacterial blooms.

Sodium sulfate (Na2SO4), Sodium thiosulfate (Na2S2O3), Ferrous sulfate (FeSO4•7H2O), Cerium nitrate (Ce(NO3)3), Sulfuric acid (H2SO4), Nitric acid (HNO3), Ethyl Alcohol (C2H6O), Methanol (MeOH), Tert-butanol (TBA), Furfuryl alcohol (FFA), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and Hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory. Ultra-pure water was used throughout the experiment.

A measured amount of grapefruit peel was combined with 150 mL deionized water in a reaction kettle and calcined at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling, the mixture was filtered to separate solids from liquids. The filtrate was placed in a dialysis bag (model MD44, with a nominal weight cut-off of NW1000) and dialyzed against deionized water for approximately two days. Through this process, a clear, yellowish fluid mixture of CQDs was obtained, which was subsequently refrigerated at 4 °C. 30 mL of CQDs solution was mixed with FeSO4 and Ce(NO3)3 in a 1:2 mass ratio, with a concentration of CQDs of 10 mg/mL and the pH was adjusted to 9, followed by co-precipitation. The product was vacuum-dried at 80 °C for 24 h, ground into a powder, and sieved through a 100 mesh to produce CQDS/CeO2/Fe3O4 composites, which were stored sealed.

The study implemented an experimental configuration utilizing a UTP1306S direct current (DC) power source (UNI-T, China) in combination with a PbO2-Ti mesh anode electrode and a Ti mesh cathode electrode, both of which possessed dimensions of 70 mm × 25 mm × 1 mm. The experimental set-up was conducted within a 250 ml glass beaker. The solution contained a concentration of algal cells determined to be 3.7 × 106 cells/ml and was supplemented with an electrolyte composed of 4.0 g/L Na2SO4. The electrodes were vertically positioned into three-dimensional matrices of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs with the distance between the electrodes was fixed at 4.0 cm. Samples were collected at predetermined time intervals ranging from 5 to 45 min. Based on the results of the pre-experiments, variable experiments were carried out using current densities of 7, 14, 21 and 28 mA/cm2. In addition, the effect of different concentration gradients of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particle electrodes on the system, specifically at 0.01 g/250 ml, 0.05 g/250 ml, 0.1 g/250 ml, and 0.15 g/250 ml, was also investigated. Water quality indicators for actual water bodies are detailed in Supplementary Material Table S1.

The optical density (OD 680) of the algal suspension was measured at a wavelength of 680 nm using a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer. Ethanol centrifugation and repeated thawing cycles were employed to prepare the algal cells. Chlorophyll-a extraction was performed using ethanol, and its concentration was determined by measuring the absorbance at 665 nm and 649 nm (Huang et al., 2021). Crystal structure, material composition, and elemental mappings were analyzed using the GeminiSEM 300 scanning electron microscope (SEM) from Zeiss in Germany and the transmission electron microscope (TEM) from JEM-2100f in Japan. Excitation-Emission Matrix (EEM) Spectra measurements were performed using a Hitachi F7100 instrument from Japan. Fluorescence signals of algal cells were observed using a confocal laser scanning microscope (CLSM) (TCS-SP8-SYED 3X, Leica, Germany). More details in supplementary material Text S2.

The results of the study are presented in the form of the mean and standard deviation of three measurements of the samples. The raw data underwent analysis and visualization through line plots and 3D fluorescence spectra produced using the Origin 2018 software. X-ray Diffraction (XRD) was analyzed and processed using Jade software. The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) data was carefully analyzed and processed using Avantage software. Furthermore, the CLSM plots were processed employing the LASX OFFICE software.

XRD pattern depicted in Fig. 1 a, the characteristic peaks corresponding to CeO2 and Fe3O4 are discernible. Specifically, the observed peaks at 2θ values of 28.55°, 33.08°, 47.48°, and 56.33° are matched well with the (111), (200), (220) and (311) crystallographic planes of the cubic CeO2 phase (PDF#34–0394) (Li et al., 2023), and the diffraction peaks at 2θ values of 30.10°, 35.42°, 43.05°, 56.94° and 62.51° align well with the (220), (311), (400), (511) and (440) planes of the cubic Fe3O4 phase (PDF#19–0629) (Yang et al., 2017). In addition, CeO2-Fe3O4, CeO2, and Fe3O4 exhibit a high degree of overlap with CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs on numerous crystal planes (Fig. 1b), which further illustrates the synergistic integration and functionality of the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system. The presence of these crystal structures offers the fundamental physical framework for the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system. The stability of the crystal structure of the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system was altered after multiple uses (Fig. S1). Although the peak positions did not shift significantly, the peak intensities decreased, indicating that the orderliness of the crystal structure was affected to some extent. This might be attributed to the minor alterations in the internal lattice structure of the material during multiple uses, thereby leading to the impairment of the crystal structure's integrity, which subsequently affects some of the material's properties.

The SEM images in Fig. 2 a and b illustrate the formation of CeO2 photocatalyst and CQDs on the surface of Fe3O4 (Andish-Lifshagerd et al., 2024). After the reaction, the surface of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs became coarser and rougher, increasing the specific surface area and providing more active sites for electrochemical reactions (Fig. S2). Ce3+ is produced through the reduction of Ce4+ by the residual electrons resulting from the creation of an oxygen vacancy within the lattice structure (Sahoo et al., 2024). Defect-induced CeO2 (d-CeO2) exhibits a spherical morphology owing to the lattice's inclination to undergo expansive size alteration, which serves to alleviate the internal strain generated by the presence of oxygen vacancies in pure CeO2 (p-CeO2) (Chernyshev, 2022). It is worth noting that the presence of CQDs is further confirmed through TEM analysis in Fig. 2c-e and HRTEM in Fig. S3. And a clear identification can be made that crystal planes measuring 0.377 nm and 0.299 nm correspond to the (111) crystal plane of CeO2 and the (220) crystal plane of Fe3O4, respectively. The uniform dispersion of all elements within the nanocomposites suggests the formation of specific heterostructures involving CeO2 and Fe3O4. Furthermore, the absence of distinct lattice patterns in the CQDs samples confirms their amorphous nature (Habibi et al., 2023). Elemental mapping confirmed the presence of Ce, C, Fe, and O elements within the material (Fig. 2f–j), thereby indicating the homogeneous dispersion of all elements in the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs nanocomposites.

As depicted in Fig. S4, we were able to find energy bands at around 3400 cm−1 and 1620 cm−1 showing the O-H stretching and bending vibrational modes of the interstitial water molecules (Chen et al., 2023). The discernible band appearing at 1380 cm−1 was assigned to the Ce-O-Fe bond in CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs (Mukhopadhyay et al., 2020). The peaks observed at 463 cm−1 and 618 cm−1 can be attributed to the stretching and bending vibrations of metal-oxygen (M-O) bonds, specifically representing the Ce-O and Fe-O interactions, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1770 cm−1 were associated with the stretching vibration of C=O in the -CHO or -COOH groups, while the peak at 846 cm−1 was assigned to C-H vibrations (Yang et al., 2023). Additionally, the peak at 2480 cm−1 was attributed to the presence of -COOH groups (Guo et al., 2023), and the peak at 1160 cm−1 indicated the bending vibration of the OH group in Ce/Fe oxide (M-OH) (Chigondo et al., 2018). By virtue of these aforementioned observations, it is further corroborated that the synthesis of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs has been successfully achieved.

The XPS survey of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs are presented in Fig. S5a, revealing prominent peaks representing C and O elements, along with discernible signals corresponding to Ce and Fe, thus confirming the successful synthesis of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs. In Fig. S5b, the C 1s spectral peaks are observed at binding energies of 284.8, 286.6, 288.3, 289.0, and 290.1 eV, representing C-C, C-OH, C=O, O-C=O, and COOH, respectively (Yin et al., 2020). Additionally, the O 1s binding energies at 529.7, 533.4, and 536.6 eV (Fig. S5c) are attributed to Ce/Fe binding with oxygen atoms (M-O), adsorbed water (H2O), and O-C=O. The peak at 532.1 eV may be associated with the oxygen atoms in the ‘bridging’ hydroxyl group (M-OH) connecting Ce/Fe (Chen et al., 2023; Yang et al., 2023). Furthermore, XPS peaks at 904.1, 898.5, 887.5, and 885.0 eV are assigned to Ce3+, while 916.8, 907.2, 901.1, and 882.6 eV correspond to Ce4+ (Fig. S5d). The coexistence of Ce3+ and Ce4+ species leads to the formation of a redox couple (Ce3+/Ce4+) at the interface of CeO₂, thereby showing favorable performance in oxygen release and storage capabilities (Yu et al., 2023). The Fe 2p high-resolution spectra in Fig. S5e exhibit binding energies at 725.0, 711.0, 736.4, and 712.3 eV, which can be attributed to Fe(II)2p1/2, Fe(II)2p3/2, Fe(III)2p1/2, and Fe(III)2p3/2, while 718.9 eV and 715.2 eV are associated with the Fe(III)/Fe(II) satellite peak. In addition, the Fe3+/Fe2+ area ratio is approximately 0.5, which is strong evidence of the stability of the loaded material (Guo et al., 2023).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of different systems on Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation
ce:section.ce:para: In Fig. 3 a, it is observed that the sole addition of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs yields minimal adsorption and inactivation of cyanobacteria cells, resulting in a inactivation efficiency of only 5.4% within 45 min. Conversely, the inactivation of algal cells by the electrolysis system alone was 33.0% in the same time. Incorporating three-dimensional (3D) particle electrodes effectively enhanced the performance of the electrochemical cyanobacteria inactivation system. The addition of CeO2-Fe3O4 led to inactivation efficiency of algae cells at 68.0% within 45 min. Remarkably, the application of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs as particle electrodes yielded the best inactivation efficiency, accomplishing full cyanobacteria cell inactivation in 45 min. The introduction of carbon quantum dots considerably enhances the algae inactivation efficacy of the particle electrodes, demonstrating excellent electrochemical performance. The efficacy of algal inactivation by the system was moderately impacted in dark, thus indicating that the presence of visible light contributes to the process of algal inactivation within the system. It is worth noting that the optimal algae removal was achieved at a Fe: Ce ratio of 1:2 (Fig. S6). Additionally, changes in chlorophyll a level serve as an indirect indicator of algal inactivation effectiveness, reflecting shifts in the physiological state of the cells. When algal cells die, chlorophyll a is released from the cell membrane and removed from the system. There is a correlation between the release of chlorophyll a and the density of Microcystis aeruginosa cells, both of which provide insights into the extent of algal inactivation. Since inactivated algal cells may still retain their cellular structure, assessing algal inactivation efficacy using chlorophyll a concentration is generally more efficient than using algal cell density (Zhang et al., 2025). Fig. 3b shows the incorporation of CeO2-Fe3O4 into the system resulted in a chlorophyll a removal efficiency of 85.1% within 45 min. The CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs composite particle electrodes demonstrated the most favorable performance, ultimately achieving complete elimination of chlorophyll a within 45 min. In conclusion, the application of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs as 3D particle electrodes within the electrochemical system significantly enhances the Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation capability under visible light, demonstrating superior performance.

Fig. 4 a shows that the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system's optimal cyanobacteria inactivation efficacy was achieved at a 0.2 g/L concentration, with complete Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation within 45 min. It was observed that when the particle electrodes concentration was too low, the algae inactivation efficiency within 45 min was only 71.4%. CeO2, Fe3O4 and CQDs function as polarized charged microelectrodes, with one side serving as the anode and the other as the cathode. Consequently, an increase in the number of microelectrodes occurs with the rising particle concentration, expanding the reaction area, reducing the electron transfer path between pollutants and particles, and promoting the generation of active free radicals (Shen et al., 2017). Conversely, the excessive particle electrodes concentration may lead to electrode overload, potentially causing adverse effects and diminishing the cyanobacteria inactivation capacity of the system. Furthermore, an excessive amount of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs situated between the two primary electrode plates may come into contact with the main electrode and other particles, resulting in circuit current. Simultaneously, the self-consumption of free radicals and the occurrence of side reactions between free radicals can compromise the catalytic oxidation process (Li et al., 2019). During the investigation of chlorophyll a removal in the system (Fig. 4b), it was found that the optimal inactivation effect for Microcystis aeruginosa was attained at a concentration of 0.20 g/L. Notably, low particle electrodes concentration significantly impacts the removal of chlorophyll a. As the particle electrodes concentration increase, the 3D electrochemical reaction region and active centers expand, enhancing mass transfer efficiency (Liu et al., 2023a,b).

As shown in Fig. 4c, the Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation capacity of the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system exhibits a significant increase with the augmentation of current density. At current densities of 7 and 14 mA/cm2, the inactivation efficiency of Microcystis aeruginosa within 45 min were measured at 20.7% and 25.6%, respectively. Complete inactivation of Microcystis aeruginosa within 45 min was achieved at a current density of 21 mA/cm2. This enhancement is primarily attributed to the amplified repolarization driving force of the particle electrodes at higher current intensities (Ma et al., 2022). However, when the current density reached 28 mA/cm2, the algae inactivation effect of the system became indiscernible, while consuming more energy. Consequently, a current density of 21 mA/cm2 is selected as the optimal operating condition. In Fig. 4d, there is also an observable increase in the removal of chlorophyll a with the rise of current density, yet the impact on Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation becomes less notable beyond a certain threshold. To summarize, a current density of 21 mA/cm2 is determined as the optimal operating condition for the system.

The water sample was sourced from the lake at South China Agricultural University. As shown in Fig. 4e and f, the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system under visible light, when employed in the treatment of actual algae-contaminated water, exhibited a slight impact on the removal of algae cells and chlorophyll a. This can be attributed to the inherent complexity of the natural water matrix, which contains a variety of organic compounds and impurities that influence the efficiency of Microcystis aeruginosa inactivation by the electrochemical system. Nevertheless, the overall inactivation efficacy observed in our simulated water experiments was comparable to that of the actual water samples. The presented Fig. S7 clearly demonstrates that even after multiple uses (magnetic separation or filtration recovery), the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particle electrodes maintain its effective performance in removing algae. In fact, even after five successive uses, the inactivation efficiency remains at a noteworthy 87.4%. This finding provides a practical and viable solution for the real-world application of the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system in treating algae-contaminated wastewater.

Moreover, the incorporation of 3D particle electrodes resulted in a significant reduction in energy consumption (Table S2). Specifically, in the simulated water, the energy consumption recorded for the Electrolysis & CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs process was the lowest. However, when applied to actual water samples, the energy consumption notably increased. This can be attributed to the fact that the electrochemical system must contend with additional pollutants present in the complex water environment, which necessitates a greater energy expenditure. Additionally, we observed a notable reduction in energy consumption of the Electrolysis & CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system when comparing its performance under visible light exposure with that in darkness. These outcomes provide compelling evidence in support of the efficacy of the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system under visible light for the inactivation of cyanobacteria in authentic water bodies.

The fluctuation in the current curves signifies the generation and transfer of charge in the samples (Fig. 5 a). The CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibits elevated photocurrent densities and stronger responses in comparison to the individual catalysts, indicating their capacity to effectively extend the lifespan of photogenerated charges (e
- /h + pairs) and consequently enhance catalytic performance. Moreover, the UV–visible spectroscopy test revealed that the combination with CQDs led to a remarkable enhancement in the visible to near-infrared light absorption capacity of CeO2-Fe3O4 (Fig. S8). Meanwhile, it possessed a stable photoabsorption property. This further implies that the incorporation of CQDs can efficiently augment the photocatalytic efficiency of the composites under visible light irradiation.

As depicted in Fig. 5b, the zeta potential is presented on the left, and the green line on the right represents the algal removal rate. The results indicate that under acidic and neutral conditions, the zeta potential of Microcystis aeruginosa exhibits a negative charge, whereas that of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs shows a positive charge. Consequently, the aforementioned CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs can effectively combine with negatively charged algal cells and algal organic matter having negatively charged functional groups, thereby significantly improving the inactivation efficacy of algae (Ma et al., 2012). This finding further elucidates the superior algae inactivation effect under both acidic and neutral conditions compared to alkaline conditions. As the absolute value of the zeta potentials decreases, the system becomes less stable. It is worth noting that the absolute values of zeta potential were significantly lower under neutral and acidic conditions than under alkaline conditions, indicating that the system is less favorably stabilized and the electroaggregation tendency is more pronounced in neutral and acidic environmental conditions. Therefore, under electrical stimulation, the algal cells will undergo electrical aggregation. In summary, the use of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particle electrodes can effectively facilitate the algal coagulation process and achieve electroaggregation through electrochemical methods. The magnetic properties of the prepared CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs were assessed through Magnetization loop measurements. As illustrated in Fig. 5c, the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs displayed a saturation magnetization of 6.97 emu/g, a residual magnetization of 0.31 emu/g, and a coercivity of 282.43 Oe (Shao et al., 2022). The multi-cycle magnetic test indicated that CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibited favorable magnetic stability and significant paramagnetic and response paramagnetism (Sun et al., 2021). And the image in the lower right corner of the figure demonstrates the successful aggregation of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs by an iron absorbent. The high clarity and transparency observed in the aqueous solution further support the feasibility of particle recovery in practical water treatment processes. Fig. 5d demonstrates that different particle electrodes display variations in cyclic voltammetric areas during the CV test, conducted at a scanning rate of 50 mV/S. It's worth noting that the cyclic voltammetric area of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibits a substantial increase compared to other control groups, indicating its superior electroactive surface area, enhanced current response, and enhanced electrochemical catalytic performance (Qi et al., 2020). Moreover, as illustrated in Fig. 5e and f, the exploration of the behavior of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particles at various scanning rates demonstrates a linear relationship between the scanning rate and the peak current. This correlation suggests that the particle electrodes possess precise control over electrochemical rates and stability (Zhang et al., 2020). The EIS curves presented in Fig. 5g shows that, in comparison to CeO2, Fe3O4, and CeO2-Fe3O4, the arc radius of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs is markedly reduced. And a smaller arc radius indicates lower electrochemical impedance and an increased charge transfer rate, which indicates the improved conductivity of for CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs (Xu et al., 2021). In this study, the polarization curves of various materials are presented in Fig. 5h. Notably, CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs demonstrated a relatively low overpotential for hydrogen and oxygen evolution reaction. Furthermore, when deployed as particle electrodes, a higher level of current efficiency was observed for CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs, reflecting its superior electrode current density at a lower potential. These results show CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs have higher electrocatalytic activity than the control group. Therefore, incorporating CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs as particle electrodes can be an effective strategy in enhancing the inactivation efficiency of Microcystis aeruginosa. Moreover, the smaller Tafel slope of CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs shown in Fig. 5i suggests faster reaction kinetics, indicating its superior performance (Yan et al., 2023). In summary, the excellent performance of the composite nanomaterial CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs as particle electrodes are demonstrated.

Based on the results of the fluorescence EEMs spectra analysis in Fig. 6 , the reaction system shows four distinct fluorescence peaks. These peaks have been labeled as A (λem/λex = 330/275 nm), B (λem/λex = 330/225 nm), C (λem/λex = 465/365 nm) and D (λem/λex = 465/275 nm). Peaks A, B, C and D correspond to dissolved microbial metabolites, aromatic proteins (specifically tyrosine and tryptophan), and humic acids and fulvic acids, respectively (Fan et al., 2020). In the control group, only peaks A and B were observed, indicating the release of dissolved microbial metabolites and aromatic proteins can be attributed to normal physiological metabolic processes. This suggests that the algae cells in the initial state maintain their biological activity and execute normal physiological metabolism. It's worth noting that the appearance of peaks C and D was observed after 5 min. The formation of humic acids and fulvic acids might be linked to the oxidative degradation of organic macromolecules or the natural apoptosis of algae cells. This phenomenon indicates a gradual apoptotic process of the algae cells in the system, leading to a weakening of their physiological metabolic functions. As the reaction progressed further, peaks A and B gradually disappeared and the reaction virtually ceased by 45 min. This observation confirms the apoptosis process of the Microcystis aeruginosa. Furthermore, the concentration of humic acids decreased gradually, while that of fulvic acids initially decreased and then increased during the reaction. This suggests that the system not only inactivates Microcystis aeruginosa but also degrades humic acids and fulvic acids actively. In summary, the 3D electrochemical system comprising CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs under visible light demonstrates potent degradation capabilities, effectively targeting cyanobacteria and their metabolized organic compounds.

As revealed in Fig. S9a, the Microcystis aeruginosa in the control group exhibit a coherent cell structure and are distributed in clusters in the solution. Upon application of current to the algal cell solution, it can be discerned that certain algal cells remain fairly intact in terms of their cell structure (Fig. S9b), while the density of algal cells substantially plummets, thus signifying the complete loss of cellular structure in some instances. This outcome indirectly reveals the electrochemical system's effective ability to remove cyanobacteria. Upon treatment with the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system, the cell density of algae significantly decreased, and the survivors exhibited rough surfaces, signifying a loss of vitality (Fig. S9c).

Additionally, the apoptosis of algal cells was evaluated through CLSM. Chlorophyll a emits red fluorescence at a specific wavelength, whereas apoptotic algal cells labeled with SYTOX dye emit green fluorescence. The combination of red fluorescence emitted by chlorophyll a and the green fluorescence of dye-labeled inactive algal cells resulted in yellow fluorescence. As shown in Fig. S10a, it can be observed that untreated Microcystis aeruginosa predominantly consisted of active cells with a high cell density. However, the appearance of yellow and even green fluorescence indicated a gradual decrease in the viability of some algal cells within 5 min. Notably, some of these cells completely ceased their activity (Fig. S10b). In Fig. S10c the density of algal cells noticeably decreased, and yellow fluorescence predominated, signifying the gradual onset of algal cell apoptosis. Notably, both red and yellow fluorescence gradually diminished in 15 min, while green fluorescence became the predominant signal (Fig. S10d). Eventually, the red fluorescence of the algal cells completely disappeared within 30 min, and the remaining algal cells exhibited pronounced morphological aberrations (Figs. S10e and f). This observation strongly suggests the near-complete depletion of chlorophyll a. After 45 min of reaction, only a small cluster of faint green fluorescence was detected, indicating that the active algal cells with intact cell structures were entirely eliminated. Collectively, these findings validate the efficacy of the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system in effectively eliminating Microcystis aeruginosa.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of reactive oxygen species and proposed the mechanism of algal cell death
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 7 a and c, the addition of CH3OH and TBA, commonly employed as reactive oxygen species probe molecules (Xu et al., 2023), significantly impacted the algal inactivation performance of the 3D CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system in visible light. The inclusion of these quenchers exhibited varied effects on the removal of cyanobacteria and chlorophyll a to some extent, indicating the involvement of •OH in the electrochemical algae inactivation system. Similarly, the addition of FFA as a quencher significantly reduced algal cell and chlorophyll a inactivation within 45 min, highlighting the crucial role of 1O2 in the system. Furthermore, by analyzing the kinetic fitting parameters, the apparent first-order reaction rate constants for the different quenching systems were determined. Notably, the order of these constants—no scavenger > TBA > CH3OH > FFA—was found to align with the observed inactivation efficiency (Fig. 7b and d), thus reinforcing the consistency of the findings derived from kinetic fitting with the results of inactivation efficiency. Furthermore, as depicted in Fig. 7e and f, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analyses conducted under three distinct conditions revealed significantly heightened signal intensities for •OH and 1O2 under both electrolysis and illuminated conditions. These findings provide further evidence that •OH and 1O2 are pivotal in the system's mechanism for algal removal.

The 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system's impact on the disruption of the Microcystis aeruginosa antioxidant mechanism was further clarified through the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), catalase (CAT) and malondialdehyde (MDA). As shown in Figs. S11a and b, SOD and CAT activity surged in response to the system's influence, followed by a gradual decline as the system continued to operate. It implies that the system initially triggered the antioxidant defense mechanism in the cells, but over time, it led to the disintegration of the Microcystis aeruginosa antioxidant system (Andish-Lifshagerd et al., 2024), which indicated the inactivation of the algal cells. Furthermore, Fig. S11c illustrates that MDA activity exhibited an initial rise and subsequent fall. The MDA concentration peaked at 0.81 nmol/L in 10 min, after which the activity of the MDA enzyme diminished. The MDA enzyme enables to protect cells from oxidative damage. Its initial rise indicates cells' response to electrical stress-induced damage. During the operation of the system, cells progressively lose their reactivity. As a result, they gradually die, which in turn causes a decline in the enzyme concentration. This observation underscores the impact of ROS on stressed algal cells, and further suggests that the system is capable of triggering apoptosis within these cells. In addition, the concentration of extracellular microcystin-LR of Microcystis aeruginosa in the system (Fig. S11d) was detected, and it exhibited a tendency of increase and then decrease. The increase in concentration at the early stage was caused by the leakage of intracellular microcystin-LR. Subsequently, the concentration of microcystin-LR gradually decreased with the operation of the system, and a removal rate of 65.1% could be achieved after 45 min. In Fig. S12, the degradation pathway of the microcystin-LR was also analyzed.

CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs (CFC) particles exhibit dual functionality as both catalysts and electron donors. They facilitate the decomposition of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) to generate •OH, as depicted in Eq. (3). Moreover, these particles generate •OH through the electrochemical oxidation of water on the anode surface, as demonstrated in Eq. (4). The generation of O2 •− can occur either through the reaction of H2O2 with •OH (Eq. (5) and Eq. (6))(Fig. S13), or by reducing O2 using a CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs particle electrodes (Eq. (7)). The photocatalytic mechanism of algae removal by CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs is shown in Fig. 8 . CFC generates electrons in the conduction band (e
CB
− ) and holes in the valence band (h
VB
+ ) upon absorption of photon energy (hv) in Eq. (8). These species subsequently co-react with O2 and H2O to form O2 •− and •OH (Eq. (9) and Eq. (10)). HO2 • and O2 •− react with H+ to ultimately produce 1O2 (Eq. (11)) (Chai et al., 2024; X. Li et al., 2021; Qi et al., 2020). Finally, algal cells undergo apoptosis in the presence of 1O2 and •OH (Eq. (12) and Eq. (13)). In summary, the •OH and 1O2 are observed to exert significant influence within the studied system.

To enhance the efficiency of electrochemical inactivation of cyanobacteria under visible light, a novel three-dimensional particle electrode CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs was ingeniously designed. Under the optimal parametric conditions, the system achieved complete inactivation of cyanobacteria within 45 min. This system not only remarkably promotes the inactivation of Microcystis aeruginosa but also enables the efficient elimination of extracellular organic matter. The addition of CQDs and CeO2 considerably enhances the photoelectrocatalytic efficiency of the system, while Fe3O4 boosts the magnetic recycling properties and reusability of the materials. Remarkably, the CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs exhibit notably higher photocurrent densities, accompanied by smaller resistances and larger electrocatalytic areas. These characteristics strongly suggest its outstanding catalytic performance, which holds great potential for various applications. The activities of SOD, CAT, and MDA validated the induction of apoptosis in algal cells. Among the reactive oxygen species, 1O2 was found to play a more prominent role and was identified as the primary reactive oxygen species in the system. Moreover, the 3D electrochemical CeO2-Fe3O4-CQDs system was evaluated in actual water body under visible light. The results showed excellent cyclic regeneration capabilities and significant energy savings. In conclusion, this research has devised an electrochemical system that effectively inactivates algae under visible light, presenting a promising solution for future water pollution challenges.
